title: Flight Of The Phoenix

jokes.text: The narrator explains Cinco de Cuatro originated as 'a particularly vicious response by a young Lucille Bluth to the Mexican holiday of Cinco de Mayo' — she invented a counter-holiday to deprive Mexican-Americans of party supplies
jokes.setup: The narrator describes Newport Beach's annual Cinco de Cuatro celebration as a beloved local holiday
jokes.punchline: It was created by Lucille Bluth as a 'particularly vicious response' to Mexican culture — to deplete party supplies before Cinco de Mayo
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional character comedy — only Lucille Bluth could be the origin of a holiday born of racist pettiness. The absurdist logic (counter a holiday by consuming its supplies) is perfectly consistent with her character. The dark subversiveness is earned because it so specifically characterizes her worldview. The Grinch parody structure in the narration elevates it further.

jokes.text: George Sr. outraged that Rosa is taking Cinco de Mayo off: 'Oh, why can't they just call it May 5? This is what I was talking about. It's all part of the Mexican war on May 5.'
jokes.setup: Rosa the housekeeper isn't cleaning because it's Cinco de Mayo
jokes.punchline: George Sr. frames a cultural holiday as a 'Mexican war on May 5,' escalating petty racism to conspiratorial absurdity
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'Mexican war on May 5' perfectly encapsulates privileged obliviousness escalated to paranoia. It's character-specific (George Sr. always inflates his own victimhood) and efficiently delivered. Strong but not extraordinary.

jokes.text: George Sr. rages 'Where the [bleep] are my socks?!' and Lucille responds 'Now it's not so funny, is it?'
jokes.setup: Lucille created Cinco de Cuatro to spite Mexican workers; George Sr. was initially skeptical
jokes.punchline: The moment George Sr. is personally inconvenienced by his own housekeeper taking the day off, he immediately converts — Lucille's smug 'Now it's not so funny' lands the turn
jokes.explanation: Economical and structurally sharp — the skeptic is converted the instant he's inconvenienced. Lucille's punchline is perfectly timed and character-true. The beat earns its laugh through character logic rather than shock.

jokes.text: The narrator delivers a Grinch-parody passage: 'You see, the Hispanics of Newport liked Cinco a lot / but high up on Coast Highway, Lucille Bluth did not... And then Lucille had a horrible thought, a thought that was thoughtless and better to not.'
jokes.setup: Standard narrator exposition about the holiday's origins
jokes.punchline: The narrator suddenly shifts into Dr. Seuss / How the Grinch Stole Christmas meter and rhyme scheme, framing Lucille as the Grinch of Mexican culture
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional writing. The Grinch parody is structurally flawless — the meter holds perfectly, the character mapping (Lucille as Grinch) is exact and revealing, and the darkness of the underlying joke (racist holiday suppression) is redeemed by the absurdity of the literary framing. 'A thought that was thoughtless and better to not' is a genuinely brilliant comic rhyme. The rewatch bonus is high because viewers who catch the reference on first watch are rewarded, and those who don't will catch it later.

jokes.text: 'Fill the bay with chorizo. Chorizo dump!'
jokes.setup: The holiday was 'originally intended to deplete the party supplies the Cinco de Mayo celebrants were relying upon'
jokes.punchline: The specific method: dumping chorizo into the bay — an absurdly specific, logistically baffling sabotage strategy
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'chorizo dump' is the joke — it's both an absurdly real thing happening AND perfectly encapsulates the deranged pettiness of the holiday's origin. Two words do a lot of work.

jokes.text: Narrator notes the Festival of Lights is 'another phrase they wanted back'
jokes.setup: The Cinco de Cuatro boat parade is called 'the Festival of Lights'
jokes.punchline: The parenthetical acknowledgment that the Hispanic community also had prior claim to this name — delivered with complete narratorial neutrality
jokes.explanation: The throwaway parenthetical quality is what makes this work — it implies an entire history of cultural appropriation in four words. The deadpan delivery is the joke. Solid economy of language.

jokes.text: Lucille 2 is introduced as 'a longtime patron of the Bluth family' and the narrator immediately undercuts this: 'She certainly had demonstrated that with many of the men in the family... even having fallen for a few, although some of that was the vertigo she suffered from. But not all of it.'
jokes.setup: Lucille 2 is introduced formally as a patron of the Bluth family
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals she's been romantically involved with multiple Bluth men, and the 'falling for' wordplay operates on both levels — she literally falls (vertigo) and metaphorically falls in love. 'But not all of it' is the perfect button.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional wordplay integrated with character. The 'fallen for' double meaning (romantic/physical) works perfectly because it requires knowledge of her vertigo condition to land. 'But not all of it' as the button is surgically precise — it confirms the romantic angle without over-explaining. This is exactly the kind of dense layering AD is celebrated for.

jokes.text: Michael says 'I'm gonna have sex with you for money' to Lucille 2
jokes.setup: Michael desperately needs money and Lucille 2 asks 'What are you suggesting?'
jokes.punchline: Michael states it with startling bluntness: 'I'm gonna have sex with you for money.' The narrator frames it as him 'debasing himself.'
jokes.explanation: The comedy comes from Michael's painful earnestness — he states it like a business proposition rather than a seduction, which is both horrifying and deeply in character for someone who reduces everything to transactions. The narrator's euphemistic framing ('debased himself') vs. Michael's blunt language creates the gap that generates the laugh.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Later that night, after Michael did something unthinkable, he was looking for a place to hide'
jokes.setup: We've just seen Michael proposition Lucille 2 for sex
jokes.punchline: The narrator describes it as 'something unthinkable' without clarifying — both escalating our imagination and euphemistically acknowledging what happened. The prim narrator voice against the sordid reality.
jokes.explanation: Functional narrator comedy — the gap between prim language and sordid content is a reliable AD technique. Competent but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Gob explains he wanted to rub it in rather than help: 'Well, I wasn't hoping to help. I was hoping to rub it in because of all the unpleasantness.'
jokes.setup: Michael confesses he did something terrible and Gob asks 'Do you want to talk about it?'
jokes.punchline: Gob corrects himself — he wasn't offering support, he wanted to gloat. The self-correction is perfectly Gob.
jokes.explanation: Pure Gob characterization — his self-interested cruelty dressed as brotherly concern, then immediately self-corrected with total unawareness of how terrible it sounds. The phrase 'the unpleasantness' (used repeatedly) also builds a running joke about something we're never told about.

jokes.text: Gob: 'Oh, damn it, I meant to throw these in the chipper. God, I'm just not into Cuatro this year.'
jokes.setup: Gob and Michael are having a serious emotional conversation about shame
jokes.punchline: Gob is holding something he was going to put in a wood chipper, treating it as a mundane errand, and expresses existential disappointment about the holiday — complete non-sequitur that reveals his chaotic inner life
jokes.explanation: The casual 'chipper' mention in the middle of an emotional scene is quintessential Gob — he has an agenda entirely separate from the scene he's in. The holiday disappointment is a funny secondary detail that shows his emotional register is set to 'petty' even during serious moments.

jokes.text: Gob offers to take back Michael's apology in exchange for a chance to humiliate him: 'I'd be willing to let you have your apology back if you give me the chance to try. I mean, I'm gonna need some specifics about the thing that you're ashamed of. Otherwise I'll just be taking general potshots.'
jokes.setup: Michael says Gob couldn't make him feel worse
jokes.punchline: Gob accepts this as a challenge and negotiates the terms of his humiliation attempt, asking for specifics so he can be targeted rather than general
jokes.explanation: This is excellent Gob comedy — the negotiation structure ('I'll need some specifics') treats cruelty as a professional service. 'Otherwise I'll just be taking general potshots' is a genuinely great line because it implies he has potshots queued up and just needs to select the right ones. High character integration — this is only possible from Gob.

jokes.text: Gob offers 'bad example': 'if you were ashamed of being in love with a man, suddenly discovered these new feelings, then I might say something like, "Homo much?"' — 'It's not that.' — 'I said "Bad example."'
jokes.setup: Gob is explaining he needs specifics to humiliate Michael effectively and offers a 'bad example'
jokes.punchline: The 'bad example' is conspicuously specific and personal — strongly implying Gob is projecting his own secret situation. His defensive 'I said bad example' when Michael denies it is the tell.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional foreshadowing comedy — the 'bad example' that's clearly personal is a technique AD uses brilliantly. Gob's defensive reaction to Michael's denial ('I said bad example') confirms the subtext without stating it. The joke rewards rewatching because the season arc about Gob's feelings for Tony Wonder recontextualizes this moment completely. High character integration — only Gob projects his shame this way.

jokes.text: Narrator reveals Gob has been taking Forget-Me-Nows (roofies) to erase his own memories: 'Gob had always taken a pill he called a Forget-Me-Now for that... but was commonly known as a roofie.'
jokes.setup: Gob says 'Hey, I'm down to my last Forget-Me-Now'
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals the Forget-Me-Now is just a roofie that Gob takes on himself — the casual explanation of a horrifying and absurd habit
jokes.explanation: The rebranding of a roofie as a 'Forget-Me-Now' is brilliant — the name is simultaneously precious and horrifying. The fact that Gob uses it on himself regularly reveals both his lifestyle chaos and his deep self-loathing. The callback to earlier seasons earns extra points. This is a genuinely original recurring character concept.

jokes.text: 'John Beard Jr.' — Gob mentions the name cryptically as if it explains how he ran out of roofies, then immediately says 'I don't need to get into it.'
jokes.setup: Gob is puzzled about being down to his last Forget-Me-Now: 'How could that possibly be?'
jokes.punchline: He says 'John Beard Jr.' as if that explains everything, then refuses to elaborate — implying an insane off-screen story
jokes.explanation: The unexplained name as sufficient explanation is a classic AD technique — it implies an entire disaster we never see. The specificity of 'Jr.' adds absurdity. The refusal to elaborate is the punchline. This will likely be a setup for a later payoff in another episode.

jokes.text: Gob desperately tries to roofie Michael to make him forget his shame: 'Take this, Michael, forget, forget... Put the mask back on. Forget this, Michael. Forget, forget, Michael. Forget, now is the time to forget. Now you're forgetting. Now you're forgetting.'
jokes.setup: Michael refuses to forget what he did with Lucille 2
jokes.punchline: Gob's increasingly frantic and incantatory commands to 'forget' escalate to absurdity — the repetition of 'forget' becomes a comedic spell, accompanied by what appears to be him trying to physically administer the pill
jokes.explanation: The escalating repetition of 'forget' is funny through persistence and Gob's desperation. The joke loses a bit of economy through over-repetition, but the character logic (Gob genuinely cannot let Michael live with this guilt because it implicates him somehow) is solid.

jokes.text: The 'Arrested Development' theme plays and the title card reads: 'Now, the story of a family whose future was abruptly canceled and the one son who had no choice but to keep himself together.'
jokes.setup: Standard AD title card introduction
jokes.punchline: The show's return after cancellation is acknowledged in the title card — 'whose future was abruptly canceled' refers both to the family's fortunes AND to the show's actual cancellation. 'Keep himself together' after a 7-year gap.
jokes.explanation: This is an exceptional meta moment — the show's title card has been rewritten to acknowledge its own cancellation and revival. 'Whose future was abruptly canceled' is a perfect double-meaning that works on the narrative and real-world levels simultaneously. The economy is near-perfect; maximum meaning in minimum words. For returning fans watching after the 7-year gap, this lands as a genuine emotional and comedic payoff.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'Six months before the Forget-Me-Now, Michael had made good on a long-standing threat to go to Phoenix, and even though the Phoenix he went to was online, he still enjoyed the full college experience by living in a dorm room while doing so.'
jokes.setup: Michael is described as going to Phoenix — a fresh start destination
jokes.punchline: The Phoenix he attended is the University of Phoenix (online) — the deadpan pivot from 'he made good on his threat' to 'the Phoenix he went to was online' undercuts the triumphant framing
jokes.explanation: The misdirection is structurally elegant: 'made good on a long-standing threat to go to Phoenix' sounds like a triumphant fresh start, then 'the Phoenix he went to was online' deflates it completely. Character-true (Michael overestimates himself) and economically delivered. The kicker — that he's living in his son's dorm to do online school — is the secondary punchline.

jokes.text: Michael has tried 'family' as his password: 'George Michael, what is my log-on password again?' / 'I have tried "family." It does not work for me.' / 'No, privacy.'
jokes.setup: Michael can't remember his computer password
jokes.punchline: His attempted password is 'family' — the thing he claims to value above all. The real password is 'privacy' — the thing he actually violates constantly. The juxtaposition is both ironic and characterologically perfect.
jokes.explanation: This is excellent compressed character comedy — Michael's failed password attempt reveals his self-image ('family') vs. what his son actually values ('privacy') in a single beat. The irony that Michael constantly violates privacy but that's the password is layered and smart. Near-perfect economy.

jokes.text: Michael: 'We have no secrets from each other. We know everything there is to know about each other. We're like twins.' George Michael: 'I don't see us as twins.'
jokes.setup: Michael is arguing that he and George Michael are extremely close
jokes.punchline: George Michael's flat 'I don't see us as twins' undercuts the romantic father-son bond Michael is narrating — and sets up the irony that George Michael's entire life is a secret from Michael
jokes.explanation: The understated pushback from George Michael is funnier than an argument would be. The dramatic irony (Michael thinks they're transparent to each other; George Michael is hiding his software, his cousin, his whole life) makes this a setup-heavy joke that rewards full-episode context.

jokes.text: Michael points at a student and says 'Look at that guy. He thinks he's a musician' then immediately pivots to praising himself: 'People say I don't age... "Hey, look at that guy. He's got the hair of a teenager." And I say, "Take your best shot" because I think that that's a good thing because I'm self-effacing.'
jokes.setup: Michael is advising George Michael to have a better sense of humor about himself
jokes.punchline: His example of 'self-effacing humor' is actually bragging about not aging — he genuinely cannot see that his 'self-deprecation' is narcissism
jokes.explanation: This is a sharp piece of character comedy — Michael defining 'self-effacing' as the opposite of what it means, completely sincerely. He mocks a student for thinking he's a musician, then in the same breath positions himself as admirably humble for accepting compliments. The blind spot is perfectly written.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'Well, it's hard to be self-effacing when you're a perfect specimen of rugged manlessness.'
jokes.setup: Michael urges George Michael to be more self-deprecating
jokes.punchline: 'Rugged manlessness' — a malapropism or deliberate twist on 'rugged manliness' that accidentally describes George Michael perfectly (an awkward, not-yet-grown-up young man). Whether intentional or not in-universe, it's a perfectly chosen self-own.
jokes.explanation: 'Rugged manlessness' is a legitimately brilliant coinage — it's either a malapropism George Michael doesn't notice, or a deliberate self-aware joke, and both readings work. Either way it perfectly and accidentally describes him. Maximum economy (two words do all the work), high originality, deeply character-specific. This is a standout line.

jokes.text: The campus housing office gives George Michael a handicap double room because of name confusion: two staff members discuss 'This guy's name is actually George Michael' / 'No, it's two different guys. They're twins, ugly.' / 'We should sign them up for Twin Club. And let's give them the big handicap room.'
jokes.setup: The narrator explains George Michael got a big double room because of confusion over his name
jokes.punchline: The housing staff's logic is absurdist: they decide the George Michael discrepancy means there are ugly twins, so they assign them the handicap room
jokes.explanation: The internal logic of the housing staff is delightfully deranged — the 'ugly twins' conclusion from the name mismatch is a funny non-sequitur. 'Let's give them the big handicap room' as a kind of backwards generosity is charming absurdism.

jokes.text: George Michael mentions wanting to 'wean off the name George Michael' due to embarrassing search results, and the news clip plays: 'It was in this men's room in -- get this -- Beverly Hills that singer George Michael was arrested for soliciting sex from an undercover cop.'
jokes.setup: George Michael wants a new name for privacy/fresh start reasons
jokes.punchline: The specific reason is revealed via news clip — the singer George Michael's bathroom arrest, delivered with journalistic gravitas
jokes.explanation: The intercutting of George Michael Bluth's earnest desire for privacy with the news clip about the singer's bathroom arrest is well-executed. The 'get this — Beverly Hills' from the anchor is funny in its own right. Solid construction.

jokes.text: Michael suggests 'Boy George' as an alternative name, and the BBC news clip immediately plays: 'Singer Boy George in the news again today, this time for sexual assault in a men's public bathroom.'
jokes.setup: Michael naively suggests 'Boy George' as a better name than George Michael
jokes.punchline: Immediate news clip reveals Boy George also had a bathroom scandal — escalating the joke and making Michael's suggestion worse
jokes.explanation: The instant callback with escalation is structurally perfect — Michael's oblivious alternative suggestion is demolished by an immediate cut to another bathroom-crime news clip. The escalation (from solicitation to assault) makes it worse, not just repetitive. The structure is tight: setup, first punchline, second attempt, bigger punchline. Classic comedy escalation.

jokes.text: George Michael: 'I think I'll just stick with George Michael. I mean, at least it was consensual.'
jokes.setup: Both 'George Michael' and 'Boy George' have bathroom-related scandals
jokes.punchline: George Michael (Bluth) chooses his name based on the comparative severity of the scandal — 'at least it was consensual' as a grading rubric for namesake crimes
jokes.explanation: This is an exceptional button line. The logic — choosing between namesakes based on the comparative severity of their crimes, with 'at least it was consensual' as the deciding factor — is darkly brilliant. Perfect economy (the fewer words, the better). High quotability. The deadpan delivery makes it land harder. Among the best jokes in the episode.

jokes.text: Michael awkwardly tries to acknowledge that George Michael wants space: 'Um, look, listen, hey, hey, look, look, listen. Listen, look it. Are you listening? You want your space. I get it.' — unable to say it simply
jokes.setup: George Michael is trying to tell Michael he wants privacy
jokes.punchline: Michael spirals through 'look/listen/look/listen' variants, unable to get to the point — the difficulty of processing rejection from his son
jokes.explanation: The verbal tic comedy is character-true but slightly overlong. Michael's inability to process emotional information simply is funny, but the look/listen spiral goes on a beat too long. Economy docks points.

jokes.text: Michael's guilt-trip: 'They don't have Internet there, so I'll just put the law school on, on, on pause. It's not like the first time that I've done that. You know, last time I did that, you were born.'
jokes.setup: Michael is guilting George Michael about being asked to leave the dorm
jokes.punchline: He reveals that last time he paused his education was when George Michael was born — implicating George Michael's very existence in derailing his life, while pretending to be magnanimous
jokes.explanation: The casual implication that George Michael's birth is the reason Michael never finished school — delivered as a martyrdom speech rather than a complaint — is perfect Michael Bluth character comedy. He's guilt-tripping while pretending not to, and the specificity ('last time I did that, you were born') lands hard.

jokes.text: Michael holds up a college shirt and says 'You know, same size — medium. Do you know where mine is? We wear the same size like twi... We're just like twin-- couple of identical...' then trails off as George Michael stares at him
jokes.setup: Michael has just been told he is not, in fact, like a twin to his son
jokes.punchline: Michael instinctively starts to say 'twins' again and catches himself mid-word — twice — as George Michael watches him fail to finish the sentence. The cringe is the comedy.
jokes.explanation: The self-interruption mid-word is visually effective cringe comedy. Michael's awareness that he shouldn't say 'twins' but his inability to find another word to finish the thought is accurate to his character. Good callback construction.

jokes.text: The software George Michael is developing is revealed to be called 'Faceblock' — 'Facebook connects you with everyone, this blocks you from everyone.' Michael: 'Oh, okay. Kind of like the antisocial network, right?' George Michael: 'That's very good. You can use it.'
jokes.setup: George Michael has been secretively working on software
jokes.punchline: The software is the opposite of Facebook — called Faceblock — and Michael accidentally coins a perfect description ('antisocial network') and is praised for it condescendingly by his son
jokes.explanation: The 'antisocial network' gag is sharp wordplay on 'The Social Network' with genuine conceptual wit. 'Faceblock' as a name is clever. The dynamic where the tech-illiterate Michael accidentally gets it right and is patronizingly complimented by his son ('You can use it') is a nice character reversal. Rewatch bonus because this is setup for season arc.

jokes.text: Michael explains his plan: the article about him is in 'Altitude,' OutWest Airlines' in-flight magazine — 'Next to SkyMall, it's, like, the number two most read in all of coach. Well, three with the safety card.'
jokes.setup: Michael is excited about media coverage that will restore the Bluth name
jokes.punchline: The triumphant 'media coverage' turns out to be an in-flight magazine, ranked third among reading material (behind SkyMall and the safety card)
jokes.explanation: The deflation structure is classic — Michael's excitement about his comeback is precisely calibrated to be as small as possible. The specific hierarchy (SkyMall > Altitude > safety card) is the kind of accurate detail that makes AD jokes feel researched. 'Three with the safety card' is a good button.

jokes.text: Michael's TGI Friday's Skype joke: 'I got to Skype with my advisor in Phoenix, and if I don't get on right now, he's gonna have to Skype me from a T.G.I. Friday's.'
jokes.setup: Michael needs to use the laptop urgently
jokes.punchline: His University of Phoenix advisor's backup Skype location is TGI Friday's — implying the advisor's entire operation is this low-rent
jokes.explanation: The specific detail of TGI Friday's as backup Skype location is funny because of what it implies about the entire University of Phoenix enterprise. Consistent with the theme of Michael's diminished circumstances.

jokes.text: Michael's emotional journey in 3 seconds: 'Whew. 'Cause that hurt for a second. I was like, he wants me to go. But no. Yeah? No. No... I mean, yeah, I may need some privacy... and that might mean... you going.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is trying to tell Michael he needs him to leave the dorm sometimes
jokes.punchline: Michael's visible emotional roller-coaster — relief that George Michael doesn't want him gone, followed by the slow realization that he does — played out in real time with running commentary
jokes.explanation: Michael narrating his own emotional processing in real time is funny and character-true. The oscillation between 'no' and 'yes' and the eventual slow arrival at 'you going' is well-performed comedy on the page. The escalation from relief to disappointment is the structure.

jokes.text: Michael's calendar is 'stuck on 2003': 'It's, like, stuck on 2003, so I don't have "today." I need to jump forward...' George Michael: 'No, I know, there's a button that says "today."' Michael: 'Yeah, this has no button.'
jokes.setup: Michael wants to schedule times to vacate the dorm and needs help with the calendar
jokes.punchline: His calendar (and metaphorically, his life) is stuck in 2003 — he can't find 'today' — which is both literal tech incompetence and a commentary on his stagnation
jokes.explanation: The calendar stuck on 2003 works on two levels — literal tech joke and metaphorical commentary on Michael's inability to move forward. 'I need to jump forward' is an inadvertent confession. Good layering, decent execution.

jokes.text: Michael's meal plan card scheme: 'I'm gonna run in, grab a tray, you run around back, make sure you get there in time before my hand stamp dries.'
jokes.setup: Michael and George Michael are going to get lunch at the campus dining hall
jokes.punchline: Michael has apparently worked out an elaborate system to game the meal plan using hand stamps — a grown man scheming with his son to commit minor dining hall fraud
jokes.explanation: Good character moment — Michael Bluth, a former CEO, smuggling food via hand-stamp scheme in his son's college dining hall. The specificity of 'before my hand stamp dries' grounds the absurdity.

jokes.text: 'Well, I missed the hearing... Oh, it just snuck up on me, and then they called, and they wanted me there at eight bells. You know, I thought that was their 5:00 p.m.' Michael's maritime law misunderstanding — he thought 'eight bells' meant 5pm
jokes.setup: Michael missed his mother's legal hearing because he misunderstood a nautical time reference
jokes.punchline: 'I thought that was their 5:00 p.m.' — the specific miscalculation (equating 'eight bells' with a particular time) reveals Michael's amateur nautical knowledge combined with complete confidence in it
jokes.explanation: The specific wrong guess ('I thought that was their 5pm') is funnier than a generic misunderstanding. Michael's casual application of half-understood maritime terms to miss an important legal hearing is character-true.

jokes.text: The courtroom 'reverted back to the crab restaurant' after the hearing passed. Michael: 'By the time I got back, the courtroom was reverted back to the crab restaurant.'
jokes.setup: Michael arrives too late for his mother's hearing
jokes.punchline: The courtroom has reverted to being a crab restaurant — implying it was a temporary conversion, which is both absurd and a callback to the maritime/seafood theme of the whole sequence
jokes.explanation: The crab restaurant-as-occasional-courtroom detail is the kind of world-building absurdist joke AD does well. It implies an entire ecosystem of informal maritime justice. 'Reverted back' as if this is a known phenomenon is the deadpan.

jokes.text: 'Prior to this, his only dream had been to possess the power of flight. But that one turned out to be not as much fun as he thought.' — followed by 'Loose seal! Loose seal!' audio clip
jokes.setup: Narrator is explaining Michael's childhood dreams before maritime law.
jokes.punchline: 'Power of flight' → cut to 'Watch out for the seal! Loose seal! Loose seal!' — Michael's dream of flight was apparently to fly, and his experience of it involved a loose seal (the seal that bit off Buster's hand). Devastating callback.
jokes.explanation: The loose seal gag is one of the show's most celebrated callbacks, and invoking it here in connection with Michael's childhood dream of flight is both absurdist and emotionally resonant. The non-sequitur pivot from 'power of flight' to 'loose seal' implies a traumatic incident. Rewatch bonus because the full context of the seal subplot makes this much darker.

jokes.text: Michael has a detailed knowledge of maritime law from playing a lawyer in a school play called 'The Trial of Captain Hook' — and before that, 'his only dream had been to possess the power of flight. But that one turned out to be not as much fun as he thought. Watch out for the seal! Loose seal! Loose seal!'
jokes.setup: Michael explains his maritime law knowledge
jokes.punchline: His 'expertise' comes from a children's play; his childhood dream was flight; the 'loose seal' is both a Buster callback (Buster lost his hand to a seal) and a pun (loose seal/Lucille). Triple-layer joke.
jokes.explanation: This is an exceptional callback cluster. 'Loose seal / Lucille' is one of AD's most celebrated running gags, and its appearance here is perfectly integrated with the narrative (explaining Michael's relationship to maritime settings). The layer of Captain Hook's play as the source of Michael's legal 'expertise' is absurdist world-building. High rewatch bonus because the loose seal/Lucille pun is a signature AD move that fans specifically look for.

jokes.text: George Sr. and Lucille announce they're getting divorced. Michael's response: 'I want to know... [beat] I want to know about the stimulus package.'
jokes.setup: Parents announce divorce — a major emotional moment
jokes.punchline: After a long pause, Michael asks only about the money — completely ignoring the divorce. The beat makes it clear he registered it and chose to move on.
jokes.explanation: The timing is the joke — the beat after the divorce announcement, followed by Michael returning to his original question, is structurally perfect. It reveals character (Michael is so exhausted by family drama that divorce doesn't register as significant), is economical (no words wasted), and the pause is doing half the comedy work. The numbered callback to 'stimulus package' makes it satisfying.

jokes.text: Lucille: 'Michael, you never accept help from anyone. That's why we didn't cut you in. We didn't want to demean you. It's like tipping an African-American. You wouldn't tip an African-American, would you?' Michael: 'Of course I would.' Lucille: 'On a train?'
jokes.setup: Lucille is explaining why Michael wasn't given stimulus money — framed as a kindness
jokes.punchline: 'It's like tipping an African-American' — an inexplicable racist analogy that Lucille delivers with complete sincerity; 'On a train?' escalates it into apparent railroad-worker reference, which somehow makes it more absurd and more racist simultaneously
jokes.explanation: Only Lucille can deliver a line this offensively absurd in a way that's entirely her. The 'On a train?' escalation takes a confounding remark and makes it weirder — implying she has very specific opinions about tipping on trains that are somehow tied to race. The comedy isn't endorsing the racism but exposing the character. Michael's confused non-response ('What...') followed by 'Did you know about all this?' 'No, I've been tipping them' is perfect surrounding chaos.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Even a waiter?' (re: tipping African-Americans) — completely losing track of the moral point in the middle of a bizarre racism tangent
jokes.setup: Michael is aghast at Lucille's racism, but has been dragged into the specifics
jokes.punchline: Michael asks 'Even a waiter?' — he has somehow started genuinely engaging with the premise instead of rejecting it
jokes.explanation: The joke is Michael's descent into engaging with an absurd premise. Three words, high yield. He's supposed to be the moral center and he's been Lucille'd into debating tipping specifics.

jokes.text: The treadmill is installed, the door is on, and the faux paint is matched — workers leave while the family implodes. Then: 'So long.' followed by a pause and a door closing.
jokes.setup: The family is in the middle of a major financial/legal crisis argument
jokes.punchline: Completely oblivious workers pop in to announce their mundane home improvement tasks are done, cheerfully leave, and the scene continues as if nothing happened — the contrast between domestic normalcy and family implosion
jokes.explanation: The oblivious worker interruption is a reliable comedic device — mundane vs. dramatic — but it's not particularly original here. The awkward 'So long' and door close gets a small laugh from the absurd normalcy.

jokes.text: Michael: 'And now perjury.' — after Lucille says she just wants him to testify that she was 'a loving mother'
jokes.setup: Lucille explains that despite everything, all she needs from Michael is to testify that she was a loving mother.
jokes.punchline: 'And now perjury.' — Michael's flat two-word response summing up the entire conversation.
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's most quotable lines. The economy is almost perfect — two words that reframe the entire conversation and indict Lucille simultaneously. Michael's deadpan delivery implies he's so exhausted by his family's requests that perjury is just one more item on the list. The pause before it lands makes it perfectly structured.

jokes.text: Narrator: 'He simply traded his shares of the Bluth company to Lucille 2. Getting himself out, and giving the balance of power to a woman who had no power of balance.'
jokes.setup: Michael arranges a deal with Lucille 2 to get out of the family business
jokes.punchline: 'A woman who had no power of balance' — the wordplay on 'balance of power' (political/corporate) vs. Lucille 2's literal inability to balance (vertigo) is the classic AD narrator double-meaning
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional writing. 'The balance of power to a woman who had no power of balance' is a perfectly constructed chiasmus that operates on two levels simultaneously — the corporate/political meaning and the physical. It's the kind of line that makes you rewind. The narrator's deadpan delivery makes it land harder. Near-perfect economy and structure.

jokes.text: Michael dramatically announces 'You know what, I am done with this family' — and then the narrator immediately notes: 'But he did anyway.'
jokes.setup: The narrator says 'for the first time, he didn't feel he needed to tell his family he was done with them'
jokes.punchline: 'But he did anyway' — the narrator undercuts Michael's supposed growth immediately. This is a long-running AD gag about Michael constantly threatening to leave.
jokes.explanation: The narrator's 'But he did anyway' is surgically timed and character-defining. It's funny both as a standalone beat and as a callback to three seasons of Michael's empty exit threats. The structure (narrator setup, character does it anyway, narrator punctures it) is AD at its most efficient.

jokes.text: A realtor visits Sudden Valley and observes: 'Maybe I can give you a tip. Might want to put a road up to this place.' Michael: 'Well, we paved the neighborhood. Just waiting for the city to build a road to it.'
jokes.setup: Michael has finished his housing development
jokes.punchline: The development has no road connecting it to the city — Michael paved internal streets but never considered access. Classic Bluth oversight.
jokes.explanation: Classic Bluth family incompetence comedy — the specific detail of a fully paved subdivision with no road access is the kind of absurd-but-plausible failure that defines the show. 'Just waiting for the city to build a road' as a business plan is perfectly deluded.

jokes.text: The narrator teases disaster: 'Michael truly felt that nothing could go wrong. And then something did. Not that. That happens all the time. This.' — followed by a housing market collapse news segment
jokes.setup: Classic AD narrator setup: things are looking up
jokes.punchline: 'Not that. That happens all the time.' — whatever we see first is so mundane it doesn't count; the real disaster is the housing market collapse. The casual dismissal of some unseen catastrophe is the joke.
jokes.explanation: The 'not that' misdirection is a sharp visual/editing gag that requires something disastrous to appear on screen before being dismissed. The casual 'that happens all the time' implies a continuous background level of catastrophe in Michael's life. Good narrator voice comedy.

jokes.text: Visual gag: Michael's ghost-town housing development — a tumbleweed or equivalent sign of abandonment visible as he wanders the empty streets he built
jokes.setup: The housing market collapses; Michael is left in Sudden Valley
jokes.punchline: The narrator says 'Michael suddenly found himself living in a ghost town he himself built' — and the visual presumably shows the completely empty, perfectly constructed but unpopulated development
jokes.explanation: The visual of Michael rattling around an empty development he built is strong conceptual comedy — his triumph turned into its opposite. The narrator line 'ghost town he himself built' is economical and damning.

jokes.text: 'Well, that's not a great sign.' — Michael observes something ominous in the ghost town
jokes.setup: Michael is living alone in his empty development
jokes.punchline: The massive understatement — a serious sign of failure described as 'not a great sign' in Michael's flat register
jokes.explanation: Functional deadpan understatement. The economy is perfect (four words) but the concept isn't particularly original — it's a reliable type of AD narrator/character joke.

jokes.text: 'This is why you don't confide in a competitor.' — Michael reacts to what appears to be a betrayal shown on a news segment
jokes.setup: Michael apparently shared business information with someone who competed with him
jokes.punchline: His business lesson from the disaster is phrased as a generic aphorism — false wisdom delivered with misplaced confidence
jokes.explanation: The character speaks a lesson he's clearly never going to learn, delivered as if it's newly minted wisdom. Competent character beat.

jokes.text: Michael arrives for Thanksgiving at George Michael's dorm: 'I thought we'd have Thanksgiving here for your senior year.' / 'I don't have a kitchen.' / 'Oh, these are my clothes.'
jokes.setup: Michael arrives at the dorm for Thanksgiving with what appear to be suitcases
jokes.punchline: George Michael objects that there's no kitchen; Michael's response about 'my clothes' reveals he's brought luggage, not food — he's moving in
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Michael brought luggage not food is a compact misdirection. 'Oh, these are my clothes' as an answer to a kitchen objection is perfectly wrong-footed. The implication (he's moving in under cover of Thanksgiving) is very Michael.

jokes.text: 'Pete died.' — Michael announces this after the clothes revelation, as if it's a relevant explanation
jokes.setup: George Michael is processing that Michael has brought his clothes to the dorm
jokes.punchline: Michael mentions Pete's death as if it contextualizes or justifies his presence — it doesn't, but the non-sequitur solemnity is the joke
jokes.explanation: Two words doing enormous work. The deadpan non-sequitur pivot to Pete's death — offered as context that explains nothing — is a perfect beat. Economy score is nearly perfect. 'Pete died.' will get a laugh every time.

jokes.text: Trang's grandfather living in the dorm: 'Don't go through my stuff, Granddad.' / 'Hey, who do you think paid for your stuff?'
jokes.setup: The narrator says fathers in dorms 'wasn't unusual' — we see evidence
jokes.punchline: A glimpse of another grandfather-in-dorm situation establishes this is genuinely a phenomenon, with its own domestic power dynamics
jokes.explanation: The brief glimpse of another elderly relative asserting financial leverage in a dorm room is funny and efficiently establishes the bit. 'Who do you think paid for your stuff' is a recognizable parental refrain in an absurd context.

jokes.text: P-Hound's roommate says 'There's a computer lab?' simultaneously with Michael saying 'There's a computer lab?'
jokes.setup: Someone mentions there's a computer lab — George Michael has been going there for privacy
jokes.punchline: Michael and P-Hound (the roommate) say the same thing simultaneously — both are equally clueless about basic campus infrastructure, establishing their parallel uselessness
jokes.explanation: The simultaneous line establishes a comedic equivalence between Michael and P-Hound that will pay off in the voting sequence. Efficient setup joke.

jokes.text: George Michael's roommate P-Hound suggests a vote on who stays in the dorm room — which Michael immediately co-opts into a political strategy session
jokes.setup: P-Hound suggests a democratic vote on who should stay
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately analyzes it as a political contest — 'Two against one, right?' — treating a dorm room vote as if it's a high-stakes election
jokes.explanation: Michael's political over-analysis of a trivial vote is character-consistent. Setup for the elaborate voting comedy sequence to follow.

jokes.text: Maeby is revealed to be in the dorm: 'There is a kind of girl here.' / 'Didn't say it was your cousin.' / 'Well, he doesn't know.' / 'Now, that's smart. Let him think there's a girl in here.'
jokes.setup: Michael walked in on George Michael with a girl; turns out it was his cousin Maeby
jokes.punchline: Michael's response is not horror but strategic calculation — 'Now, that's smart. Let him think there's a girl in here.' — the cousin situation is less concerning to him than the optics
jokes.explanation: The dark comedy of Michael enthusiastically endorsing the 'girl in the room' cover story without grasping (or caring) that it's his cousin is classic AD. Maeby's 'I'm not attracted to him' / 'Why's that even an issue?' adds another layer of cringe.

jokes.text: Michael: 'Wow, you think, you think that he'd vote against me?' — genuinely surprised that P-Hound would vote against him in a vote where Michael is voting against himself
jokes.setup: George Michael points out that if Michael votes against himself and P-Hound also votes against Michael, it's a tie
jokes.punchline: Michael is genuinely surprised and hurt that P-Hound might vote against him — despite having just voted against himself
jokes.explanation: The logical impossibility of Michael being hurt by a vote he himself is casting reveals his complete inability to hold two ideas simultaneously. Strong character-specific comedy.

jokes.text: Michael's voting scheme breaks down mathematically — Maeby correctly points out the math error that produces a tie
jokes.setup: Michael presents his 'simple algebra' plan for the dorm vote as foolproof
jokes.punchline: Maeby immediately identifies that the plan produces a 2-2 tie, not a decisive outcome. Michael's response: 'Won't that make it a tie? Briefly.' — he had not accounted for this
jokes.explanation: Classic Michael self-defeat. He calls his plan 'simple algebra' before revealing it's mathematically wrong. 'Briefly' as his acknowledgment that there'll be a tie is perfectly deflating — he waves away the flaw rather than fixing it. Good structure and character integration.

jokes.text: The planning continues through missed organic chemistry class: 'So we got two scenarios. It's either two votes against P-Hound, one vote against Maeby, one against me, with that vote being my own; or if it's two votes against me, then clearly P-Hound is being nasty, that triggers a recount, and then it's a P-Hound blitz.'
jokes.setup: The narrator notes the planning has continued through lunch and now through organic chemistry
jokes.punchline: Michael's dorm room vote has expanded into a multi-scenario strategic analysis with coalition tactics ('P-Hound blitz') — the absurdity of treating a four-person vote as geopolitics
jokes.explanation: The escalation from simple problem to elaborate strategic analysis is funny and character-true. 'P-Hound blitz' is a delightful coinage. The continuity gag (through lunch, through organic chem) amplifies the absurdity.

jokes.text: George Michael tries to say 'Dad, you know, I've really loved living with you...' and Michael immediately cuts him off: 'No talking. No talking once the votes come in. You're just right out the door. That's the missing piece. That's it. This plan is foolproof.'
jokes.setup: George Michael is trying to honestly communicate with Michael
jokes.punchline: Michael preemptively silences him because he's discovered the 'missing piece' (no talking after the vote) — accidentally cutting off the exact kind of honest conversation he always claims to want
jokes.explanation: The irony is exquisite — Michael is so focused on his clever system that he silences the honest moment he claims to always want from his son. 'That's the missing piece' delivered with triumph while cutting off genuine communication is perfect Michael Bluth. Structurally tight and character-integrated.

jokes.text: The vote begins: Michael reads first vote — 'Michael' — then: 'Huh, what are you gonna do?' Second vote: '...Michael.' Narrator: 'Michael expected this, but didn't expect the sting. Wow.'
jokes.setup: The elaborate foolproof voting plan is enacted; Michael reads first
jokes.punchline: His own vote for himself — which he engineered — still visibly stings. 'Wow.' The narrative observation that he 'didn't expect the sting' is the comedy of self-inflicted wounds.
jokes.explanation: The psychology of Michael being stung by the vote he himself cast is both funny and genuinely sad — the show walks this line perfectly. 'Wow.' as his only reaction to the second Michael vote is a great single-word beat. Good escalation structure.

jokes.text: Third vote: '...Michael.' Followed by a long pause. Then Michael asks: 'Did you mean to write "George Michael"?'
jokes.setup: The vote is now 3-0 against Michael; he only expected 1 vote against himself (his own)
jokes.punchline: Michael's response to a unanimous 3rd vote against him is to suggest it might be a transcription error — asking if they meant 'George Michael' instead
jokes.explanation: This is the standout joke of the episode. Michael's desperate suggestion that the third vote might have been meant for 'George Michael' — as if George Michael and Michael are easily confused — is a perfectly calibrated self-delusion. It's both pitiable and hilarious. The economy is perfect (one question), the character integration is maximum (only Michael would attempt this particular face-save), and the structure delivers a genuine escalating laugh from the vote sequence. 'Did you mean to write George Michael?' belongs in the AD highlight reel.

jokes.text: The narrator wonders why Michael opens the fourth ballot: 'Was it out of hope or confusion?' — and whatever is on it is worse than three Michael votes
jokes.setup: Three votes have gone against Michael in a four-person vote; he has been unanimously rejected
jokes.punchline: The narrator questions why he opens the fourth ballot — suggesting it can't help him — and the implication that what's inside is somehow worse than three rejection votes
jokes.explanation: The narrator's 'Was it out of hope or confusion?' is pitch-perfect — either motivation is equally sad. The setup for whatever worse thing is in the fourth ballot (presumably more votes against Michael, or a note, or something that makes his rejection more personal) is well-teased.

jokes.text: Visual gag: Michael reads the fourth ballot and is utterly destroyed — we see only his face falling. Long pause. Then he does 'the only thing he could think of to save face.' More silence. Then he leaves.
jokes.setup: The narrator has teased that the fourth ballot is somehow worse than three rejection votes
jokes.punchline: Whatever is written causes a devastating reaction; Michael's attempt to 'save face' is apparently a visible physical performance that we witness in silence
jokes.explanation: The extended silence and reaction beat here is doing significant comedy work — the audience is watching a man disintegrate in real time, which is both funny and sad. The 'save face' action that we see but can't describe from transcript alone is the kind of physical comedy AD does well. High impact because the buildup has been extensive.

jokes.text: Michael goes to the airport to get a copy of Altitude magazine and tries to explain himself to the ticket agent: 'I was mistakenly voted out of a four-person housing situation, uh, in a pack-first, no-talking-after scenario. Does that clear it up for you?'
jokes.setup: Michael shows up at the airport with suitcases but says he's not flying
jokes.punchline: His explanation — the entire elaborate voting scheme condensed into jargon ('pack-first, no-talking-after scenario') delivered to a confused ticket agent — is funnier for how much context it requires and how little it provides
jokes.explanation: Michael describing his humiliation in the clinical language of his own overcomplicated system to a stranger who has no context is a perfect cringe moment. 'Does that clear it up for you?' delivered in good faith is the punch. The agent's follow-up 'What do you mean by mistakenly?' is also sharp.

jokes.text: Ticket agent: 'That leaves three other votes.' — after Michael explains he voted against himself to spare his son
jokes.setup: Michael explains he voted against himself to spare his son embarrassment
jokes.punchline: The ticket agent does the simple math that Michael apparently hasn't: there are still three other votes against him that need explaining
jokes.explanation: The ticket agent as straight man is well-used here — the cold math delivery ('that leaves three other votes') punctures Michael's self-justifying narrative efficiently. Good economy.

jokes.text: Ticket agent refuses to give Michael the magazine: 'We don't even give out honey nuts, so we're not gonna give you a magazine.' Michael: 'Who wants honey nuts?' / 'I don't want the honey nuts.'
jokes.setup: Michael just wants a copy of the magazine he's featured in
jokes.punchline: The airline's hierarchy of items they won't give out (honey nuts > magazine) is an accurate observation about airline penny-pinching, and Michael's baffled rejection of the honey nut strawman is the punchline
jokes.explanation: The honey nuts line is a sharp observational joke about airline cost-cutting that also weirdly functions as a specific insult to Michael. 'Who wants honey nuts?' shows Michael taking the bait. Good compact exchange.

jokes.text: Ticket agent: 'Oh, okay, yeah, sure, we just, uh, let people walk onto the plane all the time. Do you want a box cutter also?'
jokes.setup: Michael suggests he could 'just jump onto the plane, grab a magazine'
jokes.punchline: The ticket agent's sarcastic 'do you want a box cutter also?' — a 9/11 reference delivered with maximum deadpan
jokes.explanation: The box cutter reference is dark but efficiently deployed. The ticket agent's dry delivery makes it work more than the concept alone. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Michael buys a ticket to 'your cheapest destination' — Pittsburgh. Then asks for 'the second cheapest.'
jokes.setup: Michael doesn't actually want to fly anywhere but needs a boarding pass to get the magazine
jokes.punchline: Pittsburgh as cheapest destination is a gentle joke at Pittsburgh's expense; 'what's the second cheapest?' reveals Michael's immediate buyer's remorse about Pittsburgh specifically
jokes.explanation: The Pittsburgh beat is a reliable soft-city-joke that lands on its specificity. 'Second cheapest?' is a good button that reveals Michael's priorities. Functional comedy.

jokes.text: At security: 'But you've got nothing in your luggage.' / 'I wasn't supposed to be traveling today. I told you, it was a voting situation, there was a miscalculation.' / 'Well, I mean, obviously P-Hound's gonna vote against you.'
jokes.setup: Michael is going through security with empty luggage
jokes.punchline: The TSA agent — who has heard enough to render judgment — casually opines that 'obviously P-Hound's gonna vote against you' — a total stranger offering unsolicited social analysis at airport security
jokes.explanation: The TSA agent offering an informed opinion on Michael's dorm vote is an absurd escalation of the airport stranger-as-Greek-chorus bit. 'Obviously P-Hound's gonna vote against you' is both a perfect put-down and the correct analysis. Good structure: Michael explains, stranger immediately sees through him.

jokes.text: 'These three people with burkas just walked right by you.' — Michael redirects the TSA agent's attention to missed security threats while defending himself
jokes.setup: The TSA agent is focused on Michael's empty luggage rather than actual security threats
jokes.punchline: Michael weaponizes racial profiling complaints to escape a conversation about his personal failures — the dark absurdity of using security theater as a deflection
jokes.explanation: The deflection via security-panic is dark and character-revealing — Michael's willingness to invoke fear of Muslim women to escape his embarrassment is a perfect satire of post-9/11 airport paranoia. Somewhat dated but the character logic is intact.

jokes.text: Michael is assigned a seat without a seat-back pocket — no magazine. He has to ask four passengers back before finding one.
jokes.setup: Michael bought a plane ticket purely to access Altitude magazine
jokes.punchline: His seat has no pocket — the one thing the ticket guaranteed him access to is inaccessible; he has to beg passengers for their copy
jokes.explanation: The sustained escalation of Michael's quest for the magazine — now requiring going four rows back — is good situational comedy. The absurd persistence pays off in the embarrassing pose reveal coming next.

jokes.text: Michael 'kicked himself for a pose he thought would express humility to Sanjiv, the Altitude photographer' — implying a deeply embarrassing photo in the magazine
jokes.setup: Michael has finally gotten a copy of the magazine featuring himself
jokes.punchline: The narrator reveals the photo Michael chose to convey 'humility' was a pose he now regrets — the gap between Michael's self-image and reality made visual
jokes.explanation: The narrator describing a humiliating photo as Michael's attempt at 'humility' is excellent character comedy — we don't see the photo but the framing tells us everything. The name 'Sanjiv' adds a detail of specificity. Strong rewatch bonus because the photo presumably becomes clear on screen.

jokes.text: Michael steps out of the Phoenix airport: 'Hope you know a good place to live because I'm... Ooh, dear Lord.' The narrator: 'Whoa, Mother of God! Dear Lord! Whoo!'
jokes.setup: Michael is beginning his fresh start in Phoenix
jokes.punchline: The heat of Phoenix immediately hits him — a physical comedy reaction to stepping from air conditioning into Arizona summer heat. The narrator's escalating religious exclamations are the comedic voice-over accompaniment.
jokes.explanation: The heat gag is reliable physical comedy with good escalation in the narrator's reactions. 'Gentlemen, start your engines' as an absurd mid-reaction aside adds a layer. Not the most original joke but well-executed with the narrator voice.

jokes.text: Michael returns to the model home at the end. He sees someone: 'Buster.' / 'No... no, Gob.' / 'Oh! It was Lindsay.' — three wrong guesses in a row
jokes.setup: Michael arrives at the model home to begin his new life — he sees a figure
jokes.punchline: He guesses Buster, then Gob — both wrong — then Lindsay is revealed with an 'Oh!' — the triple misdirection is the episode's final joke, implying all three Bluths look somewhat similar from a distance, or that Michael is terrible at recognizing his own family
jokes.explanation: The triple misdirection closing joke is elegantly structured — each wrong guess adds a beat, and the 'Oh!' on Lindsay's reveal has the quality of genuine surprise. The implication (all Bluths are interchangeable to Michael, or all of them have separately descended on the model home) sets up the season structure. Good episode button.

tmdb_overview: Michael says goodbye to the family business and starts his own. And the life of the family is turned upside down when their mail man Pete dies.
insight: Season 4's reboot stumbles at 1.39 jokes per minute—weakest density of the entire series run.
